extra.message:
SQL Injection via String Formatting in Student.create

The `Student.create` method constructs the SQL query using Python's `%` string formatting operator directly on user-supplied input (`name`), rather than using parameterized queries. The value is interpolated into the SQL string before it is passed to `cur.execute()`. An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL by supplying a crafted name such as `'); DROP TABLE students; --` or use UNION-based injection to exfiltrate data from any table.

Impact: Full SQL injection: arbitrary SQL execution, data exfiltration from all tables (including users/passwords), data modification, and potentially database destruction.

Fix: Use parameterized queries: `q = 'INSERT INTO students (name) VALUES (%s)'` and `await cur.execute(q, (name,))`. Never use Python string formatting to build SQL queries.

Missing Authentication on Students POST Handler

The `students` handler accepts POST requests to create new students without any authentication check. There is no call to `get_auth_user()`, no `@authorize()` decorator, and no session check. Any unauthenticated user can create students.

Impact: Any unauthenticated attacker can create arbitrary student records in the database, polluting data and potentially exploiting SQL injection in the Student.create method.

Fix: Add `@authorize()` decorator to the `students` handler, or check `await get_auth_user(request)` at the start of the POST block and raise HTTPUnauthorized if not authenticated.

CSRF Middleware Disabled

The CSRF middleware is commented out (`# csrf_middleware`), meaning all POST endpoints are completely unprotected against Cross-Site Request Forgery attacks. Although a `csrf_processor` generates tokens and templates include `_csrf_token` fields, the middleware that validates those tokens is never applied.

Impact: An attacker can craft a malicious page that submits POST requests to any endpoint (login, create student, create course, evaluate, review, logout) on behalf of an authenticated victim without their knowledge.

Fix: Remove the comment from `# csrf_middleware,` to re-enable CSRF protection. Ensure it is listed in the middlewares array before `error_middleware`.

Jinja2 Autoescape Disabled Globally

Jinja2 is configured with `autoescape=False`, disabling HTML escaping for all template variables across the entire application. Any user-controlled data rendered in templates will be output as raw HTML, enabling stored and reflected XSS attacks.

Impact: Attackers can inject arbitrary HTML/JavaScript into any page that renders user-supplied data (student names, course titles, review text, usernames, error messages, etc.), leading to session hijacking, credential theft, or malware distribution.

Fix: Set `autoescape=True` or use `autoescape=select_autoescape(['html', 'jinja2'])` to enable automatic HTML escaping for all templates.

Weak Password Hashing Using MD5

The `check_password` method uses MD5 to hash passwords (`md5(password.encode('utf-8')).hexdigest()`). MD5 is a cryptographically broken hash function that is extremely fast to compute, making it trivial to crack via brute force or rainbow table attacks. There is also no salt applied, making precomputed rainbow table attacks directly applicable.

Impact: If the database is compromised, all user passwords can be rapidly cracked using rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated brute force, leading to account takeover and credential stuffing attacks on other services.

Fix: Replace MD5 with a proper password hashing algorithm such as `bcrypt`, `argon2`, or `scrypt` (available via `passlib` or `argon2-cffi`). These algorithms are slow by design and include built-in salting.

Session Cookie Missing HttpOnly Flag

The Redis session storage is configured with `httponly=False`, meaning the session cookie is accessible to JavaScript. This directly enables session token theft via XSS attacks.

Impact: Any XSS vulnerability (and there are many due to autoescape=False) can be used to steal the session cookie via `document.cookie`, allowing full account takeover.

Fix: Set `httponly=True` on the RedisStorage to prevent JavaScript access to the session cookie.

XSS via Unescaped auth_user Name Fields in base.jinja2

The authenticated user's `first_name`, `middle_name`, and `last_name` fields are rendered unescaped in the navigation bar. Since autoescape is disabled, if these fields contain HTML/JavaScript (e.g., stored via a compromised admin account or SQL injection), XSS will execute on every page load for every user.

Impact: Stored XSS that executes on every page for every authenticated user, enabling session hijacking, credential theft, or malware distribution.

Fix: Enable autoescape globally, or escape each field: `{{ auth_user.first_name | e }}`.

Missing CSRF Protection on course.jinja2 Evaluate Forms

The evaluate student forms in `course.jinja2` do not include a CSRF token (`<input type="hidden" name="_csrf_token" value="{{ csrf_token() }}">`) unlike the other forms in `students.jinja2`, `courses.jinja2`, and `review.jinja2`. This makes the evaluate endpoint vulnerable to Cross-Site Request Forgery attacks.

Impact: An attacker can trick an authenticated admin into submitting arbitrary grade evaluations for any student in any course by luring them to a malicious page.

Fix: Add `<input type="hidden" name="_csrf_token" value="{{ csrf_token() }}">` inside the evaluate form, and ensure the server-side handler validates the CSRF token.

Stored XSS via Unescaped course.title in courses.jinja2

The template renders `{{ course.title }}` without the `| e` escape filter. If autoescaping is not globally enabled, a malicious course title will execute JavaScript in every visitor's browser on the courses list page.

Impact: Stored XSS: arbitrary JavaScript execution in victim browsers.

Fix: Apply the escape filter: `{{ course.title | e }}`.

Stored XSS via Unescaped Error Variable in 50x Template

The 50x error template renders `{{ error.__dict__ }}` without escaping. Since autoescape is globally disabled, any HTML/JavaScript in the exception's attributes will be rendered as raw HTML. This exposes internal server error details and could enable XSS if error messages contain user-controlled data.

Impact: Internal server error details (stack traces, internal state, file paths) are exposed to users. If any error attribute contains user-controlled data, XSS is also possible.

Fix: Remove `{{ error.__dict__ }}` from the error template, or at minimum use `{{ error.__dict__ | e }}` to escape the output. Do not expose internal exception details to end users.

Unescaped fieldname and errors in evaluate.jinja2

The template renders `{{ fieldname }}` and `{{ errors }}` without the `| e` escape filter. If these values contain user-controlled or database-sourced content and autoescaping is not globally enabled, this could lead to XSS.

Impact: Reflected or stored XSS depending on the source of fieldname/errors values.

Fix: Apply the escape filter: `{{ fieldname | e }}: {{ errors | e }}`.

Stored XSS via Unescaped review.jinja2 course.title in card title

The template renders `{{ course.title }}` without the `| e` escape filter in the card title. If autoescaping is not globally enabled, a malicious course title will execute JavaScript.

Impact: Stored XSS: arbitrary JavaScript execution in victim browsers.

Fix: Apply the escape filter: `{{ course.title | e }}`.

Stored XSS via Unescaped course.title in student.jinja2

The template renders `{{ course.title }}` without the `| e` (escape) filter. If Jinja2 autoescaping is not globally enabled, this will render raw HTML from the database, enabling stored XSS. An attacker who can create a course with a malicious title (e.g., `<script>...</script>`) will have that script executed in every student's browser when they view their results page.

Impact: Stored XSS: arbitrary JavaScript execution in victim browsers, session hijacking, credential theft, defacement.

Fix: Apply the escape filter: `{{ course.title | e }}`. Alternatively, enable Jinja2 autoescaping globally for all HTML templates.

Stored XSS via Unescaped student name in students.jinja2

The template renders `{{ name }}` without the `| e` escape filter. Student names come from user input (via the create student form) and are stored in the database. If autoescaping is not globally enabled, a malicious student name containing HTML/JS will be executed in every user's browser on the students list page.

Impact: Stored XSS: arbitrary JavaScript execution in victim browsers, session hijacking, credential theft.

Fix: Apply the escape filter: `{{ name | e }}`. Alternatively, enable Jinja2 autoescaping globally.

Missing Rate Limiting on Login Endpoint

The `index` handler processes login POST requests without any rate limiting. An attacker can make unlimited login attempts to brute-force credentials.

Impact: Attackers can perform unlimited brute-force or credential stuffing attacks against the login form.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on the login endpoint, e.g., using a Redis-backed counter to limit attempts per IP or username within a time window.

Session Fixation - No Session Regeneration After Login

After successful authentication, the login handler sets `session['user_id'] = user.id` but never regenerates the session ID. An attacker who can set a victim's session ID before login (e.g., via network access or XSS) can then use that same session ID to access the authenticated session.

Impact: An attacker who can fix a victim's session ID before login can hijack the authenticated session after the victim logs in.

Fix: Regenerate the session ID after successful authentication. With aiohttp-session, this typically involves creating a new session and invalidating the old one.

Debug Mode Enabled in Production

The aiohttp Application is initialized with `debug=True`. This enables verbose error output, detailed tracebacks, and potentially exposes internal application state in responses.

Impact: Detailed error messages and stack traces may be exposed to end users, revealing internal application structure, file paths, and configuration details that aid attackers in crafting targeted exploits.

Fix: Set `debug=False` in production, or make it configurable via the config file/environment variable.

Redis Connection Without Authentication

The Redis configuration schema has no `password` field, meaning Redis is configured without authentication. The `_init_redis` function connects without any password, leaving the Redis instance (which stores session data) accessible to anyone who can reach it on the network.

Impact: An attacker with network access to Redis can read, modify, or delete all session data, enabling session hijacking for any user. They can also inject malicious data into sessions.

Fix: Add a `password` field to the Redis config schema and pass it to `aioredis.create_pool()` as the `password` parameter.

Cleartext Credentials in Database DSN

The database DSN string is constructed with the plaintext password embedded directly in the connection string. This DSN string may appear in logs, error messages, or process listings.

Impact: If the DSN string is logged (e.g., in debug mode which is enabled), the database password will appear in log files.

Fix: Use keyword arguments to `aiopg.create_pool()` instead of a DSN string, or ensure the DSN is never logged.

Redis Connection Without TLS/SSL

The Redis connection is established without any TLS/SSL configuration. Session data (including session IDs and user_id values) is transmitted in cleartext.

Impact: Session tokens and user IDs stored in Redis can be intercepted by a network attacker, enabling session hijacking.

Fix: Configure TLS for the Redis connection using the `ssl` parameter in `aioredis.create_pool()`.

XSS via Unescaped last_visited Variable in index.jinja2

The `last_visited` value is read from the session and rendered unescaped in the template. Since autoescape is disabled, if the session value were tampered with (e.g., via Redis access), it could contain malicious HTML/JS.

Impact: If the session storage is compromised or the value is manipulated, arbitrary HTML/JavaScript could be injected into the page.

Fix: Enable autoescape globally, or use `{{ last_visited | e }}` to escape this value.

Verbose Login Error Message Enabling User Enumeration

The login handler returns a generic 'Invalid username or password' message, which is good. However, the logic `if user and user.check_password(password)` means the timing difference between a non-existent user (no password check) and an existing user with wrong password (password check performed) could enable timing-based user enumeration.

Impact: Timing side-channel may allow attackers to enumerate valid usernames.

Fix: Always perform the password check regardless of whether the user exists (use a dummy hash comparison when user is None) to eliminate timing differences.

Missing Input Validation in Students Handler

The `students` POST handler reads `data['name']` directly without validating it against `STUDENT_SCHEMA`. The schema exists in `sqli/schema/forms.py` but is never applied in the handler, allowing empty names, excessively long strings, or other invalid input.

Impact: Invalid or malicious input passes directly to the DAO layer without validation, potentially causing database errors or enabling injection attacks.

Fix: Apply `STUDENT_SCHEMA.check_and_return(data)` before passing data to `Student.create()`.

Student Detail Endpoint Accessible Without Authentication

The `student` GET handler returns detailed student information including marks without any authentication check.

Impact: Unauthenticated users can view any student's marks and academic records by iterating student IDs.

Fix: Add authentication check to the student detail handler.

Missing Rate Limiting on Courses POST Handler

The `courses` POST handler has no rate limiting, allowing unlimited course creation requests.

Impact: Attackers can flood the database with course records.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on this endpoint.

XSS via Unescaped Error Status in 40x Template

The `error.status` is rendered unescaped in the page title and used in an image URL. Since autoescape is disabled, if the status value were manipulated, it could inject HTML.

Impact: Low risk since HTTP status codes are integers controlled by the framework, but the pattern is unsafe with autoescape disabled.

Fix: Enable autoescape globally, or use `{{ error.status | e }}`.

XSS via Unescaped Error Status in 50x Template

The `error.status` is rendered unescaped in the page title and used in an image URL in the 50x template.

Impact: Low risk since HTTP status codes are integers, but unsafe pattern with autoescape disabled.

Fix: Enable autoescape globally, or use `{{ error.status | e }}`.
